text: Males and females do not differ significantly in running economy or endurance capacity (percentage of VO2max sustained during prolonged running).

Females exhibit greater non-sagittal hip and knee joint motion during running compared to males, partly explained by wider pelvis, larger femur-tibia angle, and shorter lower limb length relative to total height.
Females have a greater proportional area of type I (slow-twitch) muscle fibres compared to males, which contributes to advantages in ultra-endurance performance.
Females are more able to oxidize fatty acids and preserve carbohydrate stores during prolonged exercise compared to males, providing a substrate utilization advantage in longer events.
Females demonstrate a more even pacing strategy and experience less neuromuscular fatigue following endurance running compared to males.

The female advantages in substrate utilization, pacing, and fatigue resistance confer benefits in ultra-endurance performance, but lower oxygen-carrying capacity and greater body fat percentage counterbalance these, making females outperforming males a rare exception.
